competitors.label: Difference between Array, Queue and Stack
competitors.selectionReason: GeeksforGeeks has a direct stack-versus-queue comparison and explicitly contrasts the structures.
theirStrengths: It is a direct side-by-side explainer for stack-versus-queue interview questions.
theirGaps: It is less JavaScript-specific about the dequeue-cost trap and implementation choices once the queue grows.

realWorldUseCases: Choose by removal order first, then by implementation cost.
Queue: job processing, BFS, notification delivery, event buffering.
actionableExamples: A queue written as items.push(v) plus items.shift() is easy to explain, but it is usually the wrong default once the queue grows or runs in a hot path.
followUpConfusion: Choose by removal order first, then by implementation cost.

realWorldUseCases: The GeeksforGeeks page is a direct comparison, but it stays more textbook-level than the rewritten shipped answer's JS-specific order-plus-cost framing.
actionableExamples: It lists the structures clearly, but the rewritten shipped page does more to connect the comparison to concrete JavaScript implementation choices such as `shift()` versus a head pointer.
followUpConfusion: Its direct side-by-side explanation is still competitive enough that high-level confusion coverage stays tied.
If this page needs more separation later, add one short note on when a linked-list queue matters outside array-backed JavaScript implementations.

competitors.winReason: The rewritten page now earns its implementation-focused promise by surfacing the `shift()` trap immediately and tying the abstract difference to real JavaScript queue choices.

competitors.label: what is the basic difference between stack and queue?
competitors.selectionReason: This Stack Overflow thread is a direct community comparator for the common FIFO-versus-LIFO interview explanation.
theirStrengths: It answers the basic LIFO-versus-FIFO confusion very directly.
theirGaps: It stops at the concept explanation and does not help much with JavaScript implementation cost or real queue design.

realWorldUseCases: If work must leave in submission order, use queue semantics.
If your bug is about order, ask which item must leave first.
actionableExamples: A queue written as items.push(v) plus items.shift() is easy to explain, but it is usually the wrong default once the queue grows or runs in a hot path.
For real queues, prefer a head index or two-stack implementation when throughput matters.
followUpConfusion: A queue removes the oldest item first (FIFO)

realWorldUseCases: The Stack Overflow thread explains the difference clearly, but it stays at analogy level instead of turning the choice into a production decision.
actionableExamples: It does not offer comparable JavaScript implementation guidance beyond the basic concept explanation.
followUpConfusion: The question-and-answer format is still strong at the simplest FIFO-versus-LIFO clarification, so the confusion axis stays tied.
If we revisit this page later, add one tiny diagram or step-by-step trace to separate it further from analogy-only explainers.

competitors.winReason: The rewritten page now beats the community baseline on both production relevance and implementation guidance while remaining just as clear on the basic concept.

competitors.label: Difference Between Stack and Queue
competitors.selectionReason: This Tutorialspoint article is a direct stack-versus-queue comparison, which is a better fit than the older queue-only fallback.
theirStrengths: It is a direct stack-versus-queue explainer instead of a single-structure tutorial.
theirGaps: It stays generic on JavaScript implementation cost and does not help much with the `shift()` trap or better queue designs.

realWorldUseCases: Choose the structure by removal order first, then by implementation cost.
Stack: call stack, undo/redo, backtracking, recursive traversal.
followUpConfusion: Queue = FIFO, but a naive push()/shift() queue can make dequeue O(n).

realWorldUseCases: The article contrasts the structures directly, but it does not connect the comparison to JavaScript-specific queue trade-offs as aggressively as the rewritten shipped page.
actionableExamples: The rewritten shipped page now does more with implementation comparison by contrasting naive queues, head-index queues, and two-stack queues in one answer.
followUpConfusion: Its direct stack-versus-queue framing still keeps the high-level confusion axis competitive, so that criterion stays tied.
If this page needs more separation later, add one explicit note on amortized complexity for the two-stack queue.

competitors.winReason: The stronger first fold and JavaScript-specific implementation comparison make the rewritten page more useful than a generic DSA comparison while keeping the high-level distinction just as clear.
